reasoning: The Federal Reserve changes its interest rate on reserve balances (IORB) almost exclusively during scheduled FOMC meetings. The FOMC schedule rarely includes late March or early April, typically featuring mid-March and early May meetings. Because there is no scheduled FOMC meeting between March 29 and April 5, an inter-meeting rate increase is exceedingly unlikely, particularly given that the recent trend of rates has been downward (dropping from 4.4% in late 2025 to 3.65% in early 2026). Even if an emergency meeting were called, it would theoretically be to cut rates further, not to hike them. Therefore, an increase during this one-week period is given a minimal probability.
